video_title: Why Most Investors Fail at Trend Following | Systematic Investor | Ep.404
gist: Andrew Beer and Eric Crittenden argue that simple, blunt trend-following rules outperform complex models out-of-sample, that combining managed futures with equities at equal risk contribution is the durable product structure, and that the real edge in CTAs comes from algorithmic discipline to take contrarian positions before they feel safe. They also flag a structural performance/efficiency gap among large CTA managers and a wave of multi-strategy ETF inflows (e.g., BlackRock's $4B one-day ticket) reshaping the alts allocation landscape.

insights.claim: Eric Crittenden argues simple, blunt trend-following rules consistently outperform complex models out-of-sample, even though complex models look better in backtests and presentations.
insights.speaker: Eric Crittenden
insights.so_what: Investors evaluating CTA managers should weight live out-of-sample track records over elegant backtests, and discount managers whose edge depends on complexity.
insights.quote: the simple ones are the ones that hold up on data they haven't already seen. A lot better. And that's what matters is real life.

insights.claim: The majority of trend-following returns can be mapped back to roughly four to six principal components (energies, fixed income, grains, US dollar versus everything else), implying most diversifying markets add little incremental return.
insights.speaker: Eric Crittenden
insights.so_what: Managed-futures products trading hundreds of exotic markets may not deliver proportionally more edge; a concentrated, liquid-market portfolio can capture most of the signal at lower cost.
insights.quote: the majority of the returns that trend followers collect can be mapped back to you know four, five, maybe six principal components

insights.claim: Crittenden warns that optimizing trend portfolios to maximize diversification (via correlation, co-integration, copulas) backfires in live trading by reducing returns and creating hidden risk, because trend profits come precisely when volatility and correlations spike.
insights.speaker: Eric Crittenden
insights.so_what: Allocators should be skeptical of CTA managers who tout sophisticated correlation-based position sizing; rewarding low-vol/low-correlation conditions tends to coincide with whipsaws and losses.
insights.quote: in trend following you make your money in market environments where the ball's going up, the correlation's going up. you know, you know what is hitting the fan.

insights.claim: Rob Crochet's Fidelity paper showed an ultra-simple 12-month-lookback trend model produced roughly a 1.0 Sharpe ratio at ~12.5% vol before costs, then had to subtract ~800bps to resemble the actual complex CTA hedge-fund universe.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: If a blunt model captures the signal at a Sharpe of ~1.0, the ~800bps gap to live CTA returns implies large implementation/structure drag investors are paying for without commensurate benefit.
insights.quote: this simple like super ultra simple trend following model that he built... had a sharp ratio of one okay thing had a 12 1/2 volt

insights.claim: Beer contends there is no well-articulated, evidence-based rebuttal to the claim that complex CTA implementation (short-term models exiting at 9:31, risk overlays) erodes returns versus capturing the raw trend signal simply.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: Supports a thesis that low-cost, simplified managed-futures replication products can systematically outperform expensive complex CTA funds net of fees.
insights.quote: I haven't seen uh a well articulated evidence-based counter response to that argument. Like, where is it?

insights.claim: Crittenden cites the April 2020 negative crude oil event, where his blunt short position was profitable despite a broker objecting that one cannot stay short a negative-priced market, as evidence simple rules survive unprecedented regimes.
insights.speaker: Eric Crittenden
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that robustness to never-before-seen market conditions is a tangible edge of simple systematic rules over fragile optimized models.
insights.quote: when crude oil went negative. You know, we were short that market and our broker said you can't stay short a market that's negative.

themes.theme: Managed futures + equities: the magic combo

insights.claim: Crittenden built his fund to deliver equal risk contribution from equities and managed futures, sizing roughly 100% in an 8-vol managed-futures program plus 50% in 16-vol equities so each contributes ~8 vol, because he cannot predict which leg will outperform.
insights.speaker: Eric Crittenden
insights.so_what: A reusable construction template for blending convexity (trend) with equity beta; the equal-risk balance is the design choice that differentiates his product from higher-vol new entrants.
insights.quote: 100% in my managed futures program at 8v, 50% in stocks that are at 16 vault mathematically gives me eight.

insights.claim: Crittenden argues advisors abandon standalone managed futures within a few quarters due to prospect theory amplified by benchmark-relative comparison, so packaging trend together with equities removes the line-item that triggers behavioral selling.
insights.speaker: Eric Crittenden
insights.so_what: The behavioral case, not the math, is why blended products win distribution; allocators should recognize that holding-period failure, not strategy failure, drives poor managed-futures outcomes.
insights.quote: it was almost impossible to get anyone to hold managed futures uh for more than a few quarters

insights.claim: Crittenden frames the allocator's specific fear as putting money into managed futures, watching equities rally 100% over three years, and being blamed despite diversification working, which a 50% embedded equity sleeve neutralizes.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: Explains product-market fit for blended trend+equity funds and why career-risk-averse allocators prefer them over standalone CTAs.

insights.claim: Beer identifies a distinct allocator segment obsessed with capital efficiency that refuses to sell any equities, which is the driver of full portable-alpha / return-stacking products layering 100% equities plus managed futures.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: Distinguishes the addressable sub-markets: absolute-return blended funds versus capital-efficient overlay products, each targeting different allocator preference functions.
insights.quote: there is a subset within the allocator community who is very very worried about capital efficiency. They don't want to sell anything

insights.claim: Beer notes the trend+equities combination is now being offered by JP Morgan (Yaser Mahi), Corey Hoffstein, Winton, and Aspect, validating the category Crittenden helped pioneer in a mutual fund wrapper.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: Signals rapid institutionalization of the trend-plus-equities category; incumbents with genuine quant pedigree (JP Morgan, Fidelity) are more credible than firms repurposing generic staff.

themes.theme: Algorithmic discipline as the real edge

insights.claim: Crittenden argues the core value of systematic trend following is the algorithmic discipline to enter trades before they are socially or politically safe, citing forced shorts in equities June 2002 and April 2008, shorting all energy markets in February 2020 before COVID, and buying cocoa before a ~400% rise.
insights.speaker: Eric Crittenden
insights.so_what: The edge is behavioral enforcement, not forecasting; discretionary investors who cannot stomach uncomfortable contrarian entries should systematize or outsource it.
insights.quote: the algorithmic discipline to get into trends before they're socially and politically uh safe is crucially important.

insights.claim: Crittenden states that 50-70% of trend-following trades lose money, so the strategy requires keeping losses small via risk controls while letting winners run for one to five years, and notes he held a short natural gas position for roughly eight consecutive years.
insights.speaker: Eric Crittenden
insights.so_what: Sets realistic expectations: low win rates plus lumpy convex payoffs mean investors must judge trend funds over full cycles, not quarters, and demand disciplined loss-cutting.
insights.quote: in the trend following world, 50 60 sometimes 70% of your trades don't work out.

insights.claim: Beer proposes rebranding CTA from 'commodity trading advisor' to 'contrarian tactical alpha' because the space makes money buying early (crude in January after declines, gold at 2200, shorting treasuries at 70bps yields in September 2020), not by chasing late-stage popular trends.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: A marketing/positioning thesis that reframes trend as a dynamic asset class rather than a strategy, aiming to fix the category's distribution problem.
insights.quote: what CTA stands for is contrarian tactical alpha

insights.claim: Beer argues trend following is an asset class rather than a strategy because, unlike static beta exposures elsewhere in a portfolio, it is inherently dynamic and tactical, and its low correlation means every dollar of return shows up as alpha.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: Reframes how allocators should slot trend into asset allocation models, positioning it as the dynamic complement to strategic consensus betas.
insights.quote: This is the this this isn't a strategy. It's an asset class. It's an asset class that by its nature is dynamic and tactical.

themes.theme: Sharpe ratios and the large-CTA efficiency gap

insights.claim: Crittenden's cross-database analysis found that over long horizons (15-20 years) single good strategies converge to a Sharpe of about 0.3-0.4, with roughly 0.65 the maximum sustainable career Sharpe, while combining uncorrelated strategies lifts the blend toward ~1.1-1.2.
insights.speaker: Eric Crittenden
insights.so_what: Quantifies the diversification dividend: investors should expect modest standalone Sharpes and pursue multi-strategy blends, while distrusting any long-horizon single-strategy Sharpe claim above ~0.65.
insights.quote: if you combine strategies, you take a point4 from equities and you take a point4 from managed futures, you know, you get something like 1.1

insights.claim: Beer claims his managed-futures product has outperformed every constituent of the SocGen Trend Index except ISAM since its mid-2016 launch, and ranks number three against the current index constituents.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: Supports the simple-replication thesis: a low-cost simplified approach beating most large complex CTAs over ~10 years is direct evidence of an efficiency gap investors can exploit.
insights.quote: from our start date when the starting gun went off in mid 2016 we've outperformed every single one but one. Okay. Isam.

insights.claim: Beer asserts he sees no secret pool of large high-Sharpe trend managers except in very limited cases, reinforcing his view that an efficiency problem pervades large CTA managers.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: Implies allocators paying premium fees for large brand-name CTAs may be overpaying relative to cheaper replication, absent specific manager evidence.

insights.claim: Castro Lson raises that CTA indices like SocGen Trend may understate the broader industry because they capture the largest managers including blowups while excluding successful sub-billion-dollar managers, biasing reported performance.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Investors benchmarking against CTA indices should account for survivorship, size, and backfill biases when judging the asset class's true performance.

insights.claim: Beer notes ISAM is the one manager that consistently beats his product yet trades hundreds of markets with high complexity, an apparent exception to the simple-beats-complex thesis.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: Flags that genuine complex-portfolio outperformance exists but is rare; allocators should demand long live evidence before paying for complexity.
insights.quote: there's there's one consulting firm that I've that I've talked to for years about what we do and and and their answer is always why would we do you when we can do is

insights.claim: Beer highlights Dunn Capital as a non-index manager with a meaningfully higher Sharpe and an unusually compelling return stream because its 2022 alpha was ultra-lumpy, reportedly up around 60% that year.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: Illustrates that punctuated crisis-alpha years can dominate a decade of returns, a key consideration when evaluating trend managers' long-run value.
insights.quote: if you have you're 60 or something in 2022 you carry those numbers for the next decade.

themes.theme: ETF structure and alts distribution shift

insights.claim: BlackRock's recently launched multi-strategy ETF grew gradually to roughly 300 million before receiving a single $4 billion subscription in one day, which Beer calls the starter gun for model-portfolio money flooding the alts ETF space.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: Signals an inflection where trillions in model portfolios begin allocating to a third alts leg via ETFs, a major demand tailwind for the category.
insights.quote: then boom like $4 billion came in one day. Um so to me this was the starter gun

insights.claim: Beer maps the multi-strategy ETF space as roughly two billion-dollar funds: New York Life's IndexIQ (QAI/around a billion) and Franklin Templeton's long-short quant FLSP (nearing a billion), with BlackRock now entering.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific competitive landscape and tickers for the one-stop alts ETF bucket investors and competitors should watch.

insights.claim: Crittenden says converting his fund to an ETF would add tax efficiency on the equity sleeve (ETFs rather than futures) and chiefly expand distribution to the growing cohort of ETF-only advisors and individual investors.
insights.speaker: Eric Crittenden
insights.so_what: Indicates a likely future ETF launch from Crittenden's firm, broadening access; the structural shift toward ETF-only buyers is reshaping product strategy industry-wide.

insights.claim: Beer explains ETF market makers like Virtu, Jane Street, and Citadel keep prices near NAV but take less risk and widen spreads when the underlying portfolio is complex or illiquid, so building a managed-futures ETF on the deepest, most liquid instruments keeps execution within a penny or two of NAV.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: Explains why portfolio simplicity also improves tradability, a structural argument favoring streamlined managed-futures ETFs over exotic ones for large allocators.
insights.quote: the more complicated the underlying is, the more illquid the underlying is... they will take less risk to to to try to keep you keep the price close to NAV.

insights.claim: Beer warns advisors moving large dollars (e.g., $75 million) into an ETF should use ETF desks for block trades or dark pools rather than market orders, since a careless market order can move the price 50 basis points.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: Practical execution guidance: large ETF allocations require deliberate trading to avoid self-inflicted slippage that erodes returns.

insights.claim: Beer states the average alternative hedge-fund mutual fund has been a catastrophe for investors, returning roughly 2-3% per annum after 200 basis points in fees, which he calls worse than random.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: A blunt warning to avoid most liquid-alt mutual funds; the fee-laden structure destroys the diversification benefit investors seek.
insights.quote: the average hedge fund mutual fund has been a catastrophe for investors like 2 to 3% peranom returns after 200 basis points in fees... it's it's it's worse than random.

insights.claim: Beer argues investors should make manager skin-in-the-game and product focus part of due diligence, contrasting committed boutique managers with most of their liquid net worth in one or two products against large asset managers whose staff own little of a fund that is one of a hundred.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: A practical diligence filter: alignment of incentives and long-horizon career bets predict whether a product is built to work over 5-10 years versus to be marketed and shut down.

insights.claim: Beer characterizes large-asset-manager product development as marketing-led, treating new launches as one-sided options that get distribution if they work and are quietly shut down if they fail, placing the burden of vetting genuine intent on the allocator.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: Explains why brand-name product proliferation does not guarantee quality; allocators must verify the team's modeling competence and long-term commitment.
insights.quote: if it works, they've got a product they can go out and market... and if it doesn't work, then they'll just shut it down and move on to the next thing.

insights.claim: Crittenden characterizes the current environment as fertile for trend following, citing dislocation, uncertainty, and risk premiums to collect, with the past 12-14 months profitable after the difficult April 2025 tariff-tantrum drawdown.
insights.speaker: Eric Crittenden
insights.so_what: A practitioner read that the regime favors managed futures; the April 2025 drawdown is framed as an isolated shock rather than a regime change.
insights.quote: it's a fertile soil environment for finding trends and uh there's a lot of dislocation, a lot of uncertainty and I think a lot of risk premiums to collect going forward.

insights.claim: Beer expresses deep skepticism toward AI company valuations and the circular financing in the sector while acknowledging genuine optimism about private-sector achievements like SpaceX, which he notes IPO'd at $135 with Musk reportedly gaining ~$400 billion on the day.
insights.speaker: Andrew Beer
insights.so_what: A battle-hardened investor flags AI valuation and circular-financing risk as a watch item even amid broad enthusiasm for the SpaceX public-markets debut.
insights.quote: when I see the valuations of AI companies and the kind of circular financing and everything that's going on in it... my my my natural instinct is just to is to feel like it feels to me
